title: Finding a Grav Bug with git bisect
summary: I ran into a bug with my CMS Grav . RSS feeds just broke after an update. Going to the feed URL just gave you the blog homepage. content:
I ran into a bug with my CMS Grav. RSS feeds just broke after an update. Going to the feed URL just gave you the blog homepage. Let's try to find out where that bug was introduced using our friend, git bisect.

Let's start off by first getting the Grav git source. Luckily there are instructions for deploying from a Git repository. Let's do that.

$ git clone https://github.com/getgrav/grav

Now, we can start bisecting the repository. I know that the website was working at 1.7.38. At the time of writing, there have been 29 commits to the develop branch since then. It shouldn't be too difficult to find the culprit. I also know that the 1.7.39 version is buggy. So, let's go ahead and get bisecting!

$ git bisect start
$ git bisect good 95aa57c
status: waiting for bad commit, 1 good commit known
$ git bisect bad 4dd9861
Bisecting: 9 revisions left to test after this (roughly 3 steps)
[4c762c0ac3b36ddc45ab3b3b8fba8c96a3208b0c] add php 8.2 to test (#3662)

That's pretty good! Let's go! First, we're going to install the dependencies and get the CMS running.

$ composer install --no-dev -o
$ bin/grav install
$ rm -rf user
$ cp -r ../working-grav/user .

We're using my website's configuration and plugins and pages and themes so that we can find the issue easier. Let's check if the commit 4c762c0ac is good.

$ bin/grav server
$ curl http://localhost:8000/en/blog.atom

<HTML here>

Hm. Seems like this commit is bad. We should have gotten an XML-based Atom feed, but instead we got the blog's HTML page. Let's tell Git that this commit is bad.

$ git bisect bad 4c762c0ac
Bisecting: 4 revisions left to test after this (roughly 2 steps)
[d99c84d9f8d54cdfe6aeb07fbfad1bf776197ade] empty date to avoid confusion

Let's run the composer and reinstall and redo the user stuff from earlier (just to be sure there's nothing left over) and run that cURL command...

$ curl http://localhost:8000/en/blog.atom
<Atom XML here>

Good! Let's tell Git that this commit is good.

$ git bisect good d99c84d9f
Bisecting: 2 revisions left to test after this (roughly 1 step)
[ea010f19f0194540aefc9b06a1e1e3fbbb2b2781] Fix for bad rendering of modules

And one more time... and ea010f19f is bad. Tell Git, blah blah blah, and now we have something else in the bisect output:

$ git bisect bad ea010f19f
Bisecting: 0 revisions left to test after this (roughly 0 steps)
[1fae4504a2c17a32a91f20c29b6e33d8956f9869] move account info under account section

So that means that 1fae4504a is the last good commit! Let's see what's right after it (I didn't use any fancy commands, I just looked at the git log and saw what was right after this commit). Seems like the bug was introduced in ea010f19f. The change was like, one line. I'm not familiar enough with Grav to find out why that commit broke it, but it's enough to help the maintainers out a lot. I've created a GitHub issue here.

This was my first time using git bisect to find a bug. Many thanks to Git Tower for being the first result in DuckDuckGo when I searched for a tutorial for Git bisect.

Now that my RSS feed is fixed by reverting to the last known good version, hopefully y'all will get this article on time :)

As always, feel free to contact me if you want to, well, contact me.
